1	\chapter{Acyclic resolutions compute right-derived functors}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_AcyclicResolution}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/AcyclicResolution.lean
4	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, \S Higher derived functors
5	% (Definition \texttt{definition-derived-functor} (Tag 0157), Lemmas
6	% \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic} (Tag 015C), \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses} (Tag 015D),
7	% \texttt{lemma-leray-acyclicity} (Tag 015E) and Proposition
8	% \texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics} (Tag 05TA))
9	% (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex).
10	
11	\section{Setup and motivation}
12	
13	This chapter isolates the one abstract, scheme-independent piece of homological
14	algebra on which the {\v C}ech computation of the higher direct images rests: an
15	\emph{acyclic resolution computes the right-derived functor}. The companion chapter
16	\ref{chap:Cohomology_CechHigherDirectImage} builds, from a separated quasi-compact
17	morphism \(f : X \to S\) and an affine open cover, an explicit complex of
18	\(\mathcal{O}_S\)-modules --- the relative {\v C}ech complex
19	\(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\) --- whose terms are
20	pushforwards of \(\mathcal{F}\) over the affine intersections. To identify the
21	cohomology of that complex with \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\), one observes that the
22	{\v C}ech complex is a resolution of \(\mathcal{F}\) by objects on which the higher
23	direct images vanish (the affine acyclicity of
24	Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine}), and then invokes the present theorem with
25	\(G = f_*\). This single homological lemma thereby replaces \emph{both} the
26	{\v C}ech-to-cohomology spectral sequence and the Leray spectral sequence.
27	
28	Throughout, \(\mathcal{A}\) and \(\mathcal{B}\) are abelian categories,
29	\(\mathcal{A}\) has enough injectives (so that injective resolutions exist and the
30	right-derived functors below are everywhere defined), and
31	\(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) is an additive functor. For \(n \in \mathbb{N}\)
32	we write \(R^n G\) for the \(n\)-th right-derived functor of \(G\)
33	(a functor \(\mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\)), constructed from injective resolutions:
34	for an injective resolution \(X \to I^\bullet\) one has
35	\((R^n G)(X) \cong H^n\bigl(G(I^\bullet)\bigr)\)
36	(Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution} below). When in addition \(G\) is
37	left exact --- equivalently, \(G\) preserves finite limits, which holds for any \(G\)
38	that is a right adjoint, such as a pushforward \(f_*\) --- one has \(R^0 G \cong G\)
39	(Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}).
40	
41	\section{Known results}
42	
43	The following three well-known results underpin the injective-resolution
44	construction of \(R^n G\) and are the foundational inputs to the argument.
45	
46	\begin{lemma}[Right-derived functor via an injective resolution]
47	  \label{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}
48	  \lean{CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.isoRightDerivedObj}
49	  \mathlibok
50	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
51	  Let \(\mathcal{A}\) be an abelian category with enough injectives,
52	  \(\mathcal{B}\) abelian, and \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) additive. For an
53	  object \(X\) of \(\mathcal{A}\), a choice of injective resolution
54	  \(I\) of \(X\) (an exact complex \(0 \to X \to I^0 \to I^1 \to \cdots\) with each
55	  \(I^n\) injective), and every \(n \in \mathbb{N}\), there is a canonical
56	  isomorphism in \(\mathcal{B}\)
57	  \[
58	    (R^n G)(X)
59	      \;\cong\;
60	      H^n\bigl(G(I^\bullet)\bigr),
61	  \]
62	  where \(G(I^\bullet)\) is the cochain complex obtained by applying \(G\)
63	  degreewise to the resolution complex \(I^\bullet\) and \(H^n\) is its \(n\)-th
64	  cohomology object. This is the defining computation of the right-derived functor
65	  and the seed from which the acyclic-resolution generalization is bootstrapped.
66	\end{lemma}
67	
68	\begin{lemma}[Higher derived functors vanish on injectives]
69	  \label{lem:right_derived_vanishes_injective}
70	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.isZero_rightDerived_obj_injective_succ}
71	  \mathlibok
72	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
73	  With \(\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{B}, G\) as above, if \(J\) is an injective object of
74	  \(\mathcal{A}\) then \((R^{n+1} G)(J) = 0\) for every \(n \in \mathbb{N}\); that
75	  is, the higher right-derived functors vanish on injective objects. In particular
76	  every injective object is right-\(G\)-acyclic in the sense of
77	  Definition~\ref{def:right_acyclic}.
78	\end{lemma}
79	
80	\begin{lemma}[The zeroth derived functor of a left-exact functor]
81	  \label{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}
82	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.rightDerivedZeroIsoSelf}
83	  \mathlibok
84	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
85	  With \(\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{B}, G\) as above, if \(G\) is left exact (preserves
86	  finite limits) then the natural transformation \(G \to R^0 G\) is an isomorphism,
87	  \[
88	    R^0 G \;\cong\; G.
89	  \]
90	\end{lemma}
91	
92	\begin{lemma}[Long exact homology sequence of a short exact sequence of complexes]
93	  \label{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}
94	  \lean{CategoryTheory.ShortComplex.ShortExact.homology_exact₁,
95	    CategoryTheory.ShortComplex.ShortExact.homology_exact₂,
96	    CategoryTheory.ShortComplex.ShortExact.homology_exact₃,
97	    CategoryTheory.ShortComplex.ShortExact.δ}
98	  \mathlibok
99	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
100	  Let \(\mathcal{C}\) be an abelian category and let
101	  \[
102	    0 \longrightarrow S.X_1 \longrightarrow S.X_2 \longrightarrow S.X_3
103	      \longrightarrow 0
104	  \]
105	  be a short exact sequence of cochain complexes in \(\mathcal{C}\) --- that is, a
106	  short complex \(S = (S.X_1 \to S.X_2 \to S.X_3)\) of objects of the category of
107	  \(\mathbb{Z}\)-indexed cochain complexes \(\mathrm{HomologicalComplex}\,\mathcal{C}\)
108	  which is short exact (degreewise mono, epi and exact in the middle). Then for every
109	  degree \(n\) there is a connecting morphism
110	  \[
111	    \delta^n \;:\; H^n(S.X_3) \longrightarrow H^{n+1}(S.X_1)
112	  \]
113	  (the Mathlib map \texttt{ShortComplex.ShortExact.\(\delta\)}), and these assemble
114	  into a long exact sequence of homology objects in \(\mathcal{C}\)
115	  \[
116	    \cdots \longrightarrow H^n(S.X_2) \longrightarrow H^n(S.X_3)
117	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^n\ } H^{n+1}(S.X_1)
118	      \longrightarrow H^{n+1}(S.X_2) \longrightarrow \cdots .
119	  \]
120	  This is the complex-level homology long exact sequence and its connecting
121	  homomorphism. The derived-functor long exact sequence used in the sequel is
122	  constructed from this complex-level result via a horseshoe lift; see
123	  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}.
124	\end{lemma}
125	
126	\section{Right-acyclic objects}
127	
128	\begin{definition}\leanok
129	[Right-\(G\)-acyclic object]
130	  \label{def:right_acyclic}
131	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.IsRightAcyclic}
132	  \uses{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}
133	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Definition
134	  % \texttt{definition-derived-functor} (Tag 0157, items 3--4) and Lemma
135	  % \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic} (Tag 015C, part 2)
136	  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5264--5269 and
137	  % L5594--5617).
138	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "\item An object $A$ of $\mathcal{A}$ is said to be
139	  % {\it right acyclic for $F$}, or {\it acyclic for $RF$}
140	  % if $A[0]$ computes $RF$.
141	  % \item An object $A$ of $\mathcal{A}$ is said to be
142	  % {\it left acyclic for $F$}, or {\it acyclic for $LF$}
143	  % if $A[0]$ computes $LF$."
144	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $F : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}$ be an additive functor
145	  % between abelian categories and assume
146	  % $RF : D^{+}(\mathcal{A}) \to D^{+}(\mathcal{B})$ is everywhere
147	  % defined. Let $A$ be an object of $\mathcal{A}$.
148	  % \begin{enumerate}
149	  % \item $A$ is right acyclic for $F$ if and only if
150	  % $F(A) \to R^0F(A)$ is an isomorphism and $R^iF(A) = 0$ for all $i > 0$,
151	  % \item if $F$ is left exact, then $A$ is right acyclic for $F$
152	  % if and only if $R^iF(A) = 0$ for all $i > 0$.
153	  % \end{enumerate}"
154	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 0157
155	  (\texttt{definition-derived-functor}) and Tag 015C
156	  (\texttt{lemma-F-acyclic}).}
157	  Let \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) be an additive functor between abelian
158	  categories. An object \(J\) of \(\mathcal{A}\) is \emph{right-\(G\)-acyclic} when
159	  the higher right-derived functors of \(G\) vanish at \(J\):
160	  \[
161	    (R^k G)(J) = 0 \qquad \text{for all } k \geq 1.
162	  \]
163	  Equivalently, writing the condition with the index shifted, \((R^{k+1} G)(J) = 0\)
164	  for all \(k \in \mathbb{N}\). The Stacks Project defines a right-\(G\)-acyclic
165	  object intrinsically (as one for which the one-term complex \(J[0]\) computes the
166	  derived functor \(RG\)); for an additive --- in our applications, left exact ---
167	  functor \(G\) that intrinsic condition is equivalent to the vanishing of all
168	  \((R^k G)(J)\) with \(k \geq 1\), which is the form we adopt here and which is
169	  directly checkable against the injective-resolution computation of
170	  Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}.
171	
172	  \emph{Remark (alternative quantifier shapes).} Two equivalent formulations are
173	  available: ``\((R^k G)(J) = 0\) for all \(k \geq 1\)'' or the index-shifted
174	  ``\((R^{k+1} G)(J) = 0\) for all \(k \in \mathbb{N}\)''; the latter avoids the
175	  inequality side-condition and aligns with the vanishing statement of
176	  Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_vanishes_injective}.
177	\end{definition}
178	
179	\section{Lifting a short exact sequence to injective resolutions}
180	
181	The passage from the complex-level homology long exact sequence
182	(Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) to a long exact sequence of
183	right-derived functors requires one geometric input: a short exact sequence of
184	objects must be lifted to a short exact sequence of their injective resolutions
185	which is, moreover, \emph{degreewise split}. This is the dual Horseshoe Lemma. It is the single genuinely new homological-algebra construction the argument
186	requires; individual injective resolutions (provided by enough injectives) furnish
187	the building blocks, and the horseshoe construction assembles them into the required
188	lift.
189	
190	\subsection{The horseshoe construction, step by step}
191	
192	The construction proceeds by induction on degree; we isolate its independent moving
193	parts as the sub-lemmas below, from which the horseshoe lemma is assembled. Throughout,
194	\(I_A\) and \(I_C\) are fixed injective resolutions of \(A\) and \(C\) (they exist
195	because \(\mathcal{A}\) has enough injectives), and we write
196	\(d_A, d_C\) for their differentials and \(I_A^n, I_C^n\) for their degree-\(n\) terms.
197	The middle resolution is built degreewise as the biproduct
198	\(I_B^n := I_A^n \oplus I_C^n\). All seven statements are structural pieces of the dual
199	Horseshoe Lemma (dual of Weibel, \emph{An Introduction to Homological Algebra}, Lemma
200	2.2.8); no new source is introduced.
201	
202	\begin{lemma}[Degree term: a finite biproduct of injectives is injective]
203	  \label{lem:horseshoe_biprod_injective}
204	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Injective.instBiprod}
205	  \mathlibok
206	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
207	  In an abelian category, if \(P\) and \(Q\) are injective objects then their biproduct
208	  \(P \oplus Q\) is injective. Consequently each degree term
209	  \(I_B^n := I_A^n \oplus I_C^n\) of the middle complex is injective, being the
210	  biproduct of the injective terms \(I_A^n\) and \(I_C^n\) of the two chosen
211	  resolutions.
212	\end{lemma}
213	
214	\begin{lemma}[Degree term: the biproduct short complex is split exact]
215	  \label{lem:horseshoe_degree_split}
216	  \lean{CategoryTheory.ShortComplex.Splitting.ofHasBinaryBiproduct}
217	  \mathlibok
218	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
219	  For objects \(P, Q\) of an abelian category the short complex
220	  \[
221	    P \xrightarrow{\ \iota_P\ } P \oplus Q \xrightarrow{\ \pi_Q\ } Q
222	  \]
223	  formed from the biproduct coprojection \(\iota_P\) and projection \(\pi_Q\) carries a
224	  canonical \texttt{ShortComplex.Splitting}; in particular it is a split short exact
225	  sequence. Applied in each degree \(n\) with \(P = I_A^n\), \(Q = I_C^n\), this exhibits
226	  every degree
227	  \[
228	    0 \longrightarrow I_A^n \longrightarrow I_B^n \longrightarrow I_C^n \longrightarrow 0
229	  \]
230	  as split, furnishing the degreewise-splitting data required for the construction.
231	\end{lemma}
232	
233	\begin{lemma}\leanok
234	[Per-stage monomorphism into the biproduct]
235	  \label{lem:horseshoe_stage_mono}
236	  \lean{CategoryTheory.mono_biprod_lift_factorThru_of_exact}
237	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_biprod_injective}
238	  Let \(0 \to S.X_1 \xrightarrow{S.f} S.X_2 \xrightarrow{S.g} S.X_3 \to 0\) be an exact
239	  short complex with \(S.f\) a monomorphism, and let \(\alpha : S.X_1 \to P\),
240	  \(\gamma : S.X_3 \to Q\) be monomorphisms with \(P\) injective. Then the map
241	  \[
242	    \bigl\langle\, \mathrm{factorThru}(\alpha, S.f)\,,\; S.g \circ \gamma \,\bigr\rangle
243	      \;:\; S.X_2 \longrightarrow P \oplus Q,
244	  \]
245	  whose first component is the injective extension of \(\alpha\) along the mono \(S.f\)
246	  and whose second component is \(S.X_2 \twoheadrightarrow S.X_3 \hookrightarrow Q\), is a
247	  monomorphism. This is the kernel/cokernel step driving each stage of the recursion:
248	  applied to the \(n\)-th cosyzygy short exact sequence with \(P = I_A^{n+1}\),
249	  \(Q = I_C^{n+1}\) it produces the monomorphism of the next cosyzygy into the next
250	  biproduct term, whose cokernel feeds the following stage (and, at the base stage with
251	  the original sequence \(0 \to A \to B \to C \to 0\), the augmentation
252	  \(\beta : B \hookrightarrow I_B^0\)).
253	\end{lemma}
254	
255	\begin{proof}
256	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_biprod_injective}
257	  \leanok
258	  Write \(u := \langle \mathrm{factorThru}(\alpha, S.f), S.g \circ \gamma\rangle\). To see
259	  \(u\) is mono it suffices, in an abelian category, to show \(x \circ u = 0 \implies x = 0\)
260	  for any \(x\). Composing \(x \circ u\) with the two biproduct projections gives
261	  \(x \circ \mathrm{factorThru}(\alpha, S.f) = 0\) and \(x \circ S.g \circ \gamma = 0\);
262	  since \(\gamma\) is mono the latter yields \(x \circ S.g = 0\). Exactness of \(S\) at
263	  \(S.X_2\) (with \(S.f\) mono) lets us lift \(x\) through \(S.f\): there is \(y\) with
264	  \(y \circ S.f = x\). Then \(y \circ \alpha = y \circ S.f \circ \mathrm{factorThru}(\alpha, S.f)
265	  = x \circ \mathrm{factorThru}(\alpha, S.f) = 0\), using the defining property
266	  \(S.f \circ \mathrm{factorThru}(\alpha, S.f) = \alpha\); as \(\alpha\) is mono, \(y = 0\),
267	  hence \(x = y \circ S.f = 0\).
268	\end{proof}
269	
270	\begin{lemma}[Off-diagonal twist and augmentation lifts]
271	  \label{lem:horseshoe_twist}
272	  \lean{CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeτ, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeτ_cocycle, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeβ, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.twistPair,
273	    CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeβ₁, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.f_comp_horseshoeβ₁,
274	    CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeH, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.g_comp_horseshoeH,
275	    CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeH_comp_d, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeτZero,
276	    CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeτZero_hf, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeτ_zero,
277	    CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeβ_comp_d, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeβ_fst,
278	    CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeβ_snd, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.ιC0,
279	    CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.ιC0_comp_d, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.ιC0_comp_τZero,
280	    CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.ιC_comp_horseshoeτZero}
281	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_stage_mono}
282	  There exist an augmentation \(\beta : B \to I_B^0\) and a family of morphisms
283	  \[
284	    \tau^n : I_C^n \longrightarrow I_A^{n+1} \qquad (n \in \mathbb{N})
285	  \]
286	  --- the off-diagonal components of the middle differential --- satisfying the cocycle
287	  identity
288	  \[
289	    \tau^{n+1} \circ d_C^n \;=\; -\, d_A^{n+1} \circ \tau^n \qquad (n \in \mathbb{N}),
290	  \]
291	  together with the compatibility of \(\beta\) with the augmentations \(A \to I_A^0\),
292	  \(C \to I_C^0\) and the maps of the original short exact sequence. Each \(\tau^n\) (and
293	  \(\beta\)) is produced by the universal lifting property of injectives: the target
294	  summand \(I_A^{n+1}\) is injective and the relevant source map is a monomorphism
295	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_stage_mono}).
296	\end{lemma}
297	
298	\begin{proof}
299	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_stage_mono}
300	  The lifts are constructed by recursion on \(n\). For the augmentation, the first
301	  component of \(\beta\) extends the composite \(A \to I_A^0\) along the monomorphism
302	  \(A \hookrightarrow B\) using injectivity of \(I_A^0\); the second component is
303	  \(B \twoheadrightarrow C \to I_C^0\).
304	  At the inductive step we have \(\tau^{n}\) (with \(\tau^{n} : I_C^{n} \to I_A^{n+1}\))
305	  and seek \(\tau^{n+1} : I_C^{n+1} \to I_A^{n+2}\) with
306	  \(\tau^{n+1} \circ d_C^n = -\,d_A^{n+1} \circ \tau^n\). The right-hand side
307	  \(-\,d_A^{n+1} \circ \tau^n : I_C^n \to I_A^{n+2}\) annihilates
308	  \(\ker d_C^n = \operatorname{im} d_C^{n-1}\) --- the equality \(\ker d_C^n =
309	  \operatorname{im} d_C^{n-1}\) being the exactness of the injective resolution \(I_C\) in
310	  the positive degree \(n\), which is part of the defining data of an injective resolution.
311	  Indeed, precomposing with \(d_C^{n-1}\) and using the
312	  previous-stage identity \(\tau^{n} \circ d_C^{n-1} = -\,d_A^{n} \circ \tau^{n-1}\) gives
313	  \(-\,d_A^{n+1} \circ \tau^n \circ d_C^{n-1} = d_A^{n+1} \circ d_A^{n} \circ \tau^{n-1} = 0\),
314	  where the final equality is the complex identity \(d_A^{n+1} \circ d_A^{n} = 0\) for the
315	  cochain complex \(I_A\). Hence
316	  \(-\,d_A^{n+1} \circ \tau^n\) factors through the image of \(d_C^n\), i.e.\ through the
317	  monomorphism \(\operatorname{im} d_C^n \hookrightarrow I_C^{n+1}\); injectivity of the
318	  target \(I_A^{n+2}\) extends that factorization along this monomorphism to a map
319	  \(\tau^{n+1} : I_C^{n+1} \to I_A^{n+2}\) defined on all of \(I_C^{n+1}\) and satisfying
320	  \(\tau^{n+1} \circ d_C^n = -\,d_A^{n+1} \circ \tau^n\) by construction. This is exactly
321	  the dual of the inductive lift in the Horseshoe Lemma.
322	\end{proof}
323	
324	\begin{lemma}\leanok
325	[The assembled middle differential squares to zero]
326	  \label{lem:horseshoe_dComp}
327	  \lean{CategoryTheory.twistedBiprodD_comp}
328	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_twist}
329	  With the twist family \(\tau\) of Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_twist}, define the middle
330	  differential degreewise by the matrix
331	  \[
332	    d_B^n \;=\;
333	    \begin{pmatrix} d_A^n & \tau^n \\ 0 & d_C^n \end{pmatrix}
334	    \;:\; I_A^n \oplus I_C^n \longrightarrow I_A^{n+1} \oplus I_C^{n+1}.
335	  \]
336	  Then \(d_B^{n+1} \circ d_B^n = 0\) for every \(n\), so \((I_B^\bullet, d_B)\) is a
337	  cochain complex.
338	\end{lemma}
339	
340	\begin{proof}
341	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_twist}
342	  \leanok
343	  Multiplying the two matrices, the composite \(d_B^{n+1} \circ d_B^n\) has diagonal
344	  entries \(d_A^{n+1} \circ d_A^n = 0\) and \(d_C^{n+1} \circ d_C^n = 0\) (the complexes
345	  \(I_A\), \(I_C\) being complexes), lower-left entry \(0\), and upper-right entry
346	  \[
347	    d_A^{n+1} \circ \tau^n + \tau^{n+1} \circ d_C^n,
348	  \]
349	  which vanishes precisely by the cocycle identity
350	  \(\tau^{n+1} \circ d_C^n = -\,d_A^{n+1} \circ \tau^n\) of
351	  Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_twist}. Hence \(d_B^{n+1} \circ d_B^n = 0\).
352	\end{proof}
353	
354	\begin{lemma}[The inclusion and projection are chain maps]
355	  \label{lem:horseshoe_chainMap}
356	  \lean{CategoryTheory.twistedBiprodInl, CategoryTheory.twistedBiprodSnd, CategoryTheory.twistedBiprodSplitting, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeSES_shortExact,
357	    CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeMid, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeSES,
358	    CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeSES_splitting, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeι,
359	    CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeι_f_zero, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.mono_horseshoeβ}
360	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_dComp}
361	  With \(d_B\) as in Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_dComp}, the degreewise biproduct
362	  coprojection \(\iota^n = \iota_{I_A^n} : I_A^n \to I_B^n\) and projection
363	  \(\pi^n = \pi_{I_C^n} : I_B^n \to I_C^n\) assemble into chain maps
364	  \(\iota : I_A^\bullet \to I_B^\bullet\) and \(\pi : I_B^\bullet \to I_C^\bullet\); that
365	  is, \(d_B^n \circ \iota^n = \iota^{n+1} \circ d_A^n\) and
366	  \(\pi^{n+1} \circ d_B^n = d_C^n \circ \pi^n\). Moreover \(\iota, \pi\) are compatible
367	  with the augmentations and form, in each degree, the split short exact sequence
368	  \(0 \to I_A^n \to I_B^n \to I_C^n \to 0\) of
369	  Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_degree_split}.
370	\end{lemma}
371	
372	\begin{proof}
373	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_dComp, lem:horseshoe_degree_split}
374	  These are direct biproduct computations from the matrix form of \(d_B\). Pre-composing
375	  \(d_B^n\) with the coprojection \(\iota^n\) of the first summand selects the first
376	  column \(\begin{pmatrix} d_A^n \\ 0\end{pmatrix}\), which equals
377	  \(\iota^{n+1} \circ d_A^n\); so \(\iota\) is a chain map. Post-composing \(d_B^n\) with
378	  the projection \(\pi^{n+1}\) onto the second summand selects the second row
379	  \(\begin{pmatrix} 0 & d_C^n\end{pmatrix}\), which equals \(d_C^n \circ \pi^n\); so
380	  \(\pi\) is a chain map. Compatibility with the augmentations is the corresponding
381	  statement in degree \(-1 \to 0\), immediate from the construction of \(\beta\) in
382	  Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_twist}. In each degree \(\iota^n = \iota_{I_A^n}\) and
383	  \(\pi^n = \pi_{I_C^n}\) are the structure maps of the biproduct, so the degree is split
384	  exact by Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_degree_split}.
385	\end{proof}

387	\begin{lemma}[Middle-term quasi-isomorphism transfer]
388	  \label{lem:quasiIso_tau2}
389	  \lean{HomologicalComplex.HomologySequence.quasiIso_τ₂}
390	  \uses{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}
391	  Project-local supplement (the \(\tau_2\) companion of Mathlib's \(\tau_3\) transfer
392	  \texttt{quasiIso\_τ₃}). Let \(\varphi : S_1 \to S_2\) be a morphism of short exact
393	  sequences of cochain complexes, with components \(\varphi.\tau_1, \varphi.\tau_2,
394	  \varphi.\tau_3\) on the three columns. Suppose \(\varphi.\tau_1\) and \(\varphi.\tau_3\)
395	  are quasi-isomorphisms, and that the middle component \(\varphi.\tau_2\) is a
396	  monomorphism in every degree \(i\) that has no incoming differential, and an
397	  epimorphism in every degree \(i\) that has no outgoing differential. Then
398	  \(\varphi.\tau_2\) is a quasi-isomorphism.
399	\end{lemma}
400	
401	\begin{proof}
402	  \uses{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}
403	  This is the homology four-lemma. Fix a degree \(i\); we show
404	  \(H^i(\varphi.\tau_2)\) is an isomorphism by proving it is both epimorphic and
405	  monomorphic and concluding that a map which is both is an isomorphism. \emph{Epi:}
406	  when \(i\) has an outgoing differential to some \(j\), the two short exact sequences
407	  yield a commuting ladder whose rows are the five-term exact windows of the homology
408	  long exact sequence (Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) about degree
409	  \(i\); the four-lemma (epi form), using that \(H^i(\varphi.\tau_1)\),
410	  \(H^i(\varphi.\tau_3)\) are epi and \(H^j(\varphi.\tau_1)\) is mono, forces
411	  \(H^i(\varphi.\tau_2)\) epi. When \(i\) has no outgoing differential,
412	  \(\varphi.\tau_2\) is epi in degree \(i\) by hypothesis, and an epimorphism of
413	  complexes that is epi in a degree with no outgoing differential is epi on homology
414	  there. \emph{Mono:} dually, when \(i\) has an incoming differential from some \(k\),
415	  the four-lemma (mono form), using that \(H^k(\varphi.\tau_3)\) is epi and
416	  \(H^i(\varphi.\tau_1), H^i(\varphi.\tau_3)\) are mono, forces
417	  \(H^i(\varphi.\tau_2)\) mono; when \(i\) has no incoming differential it is mono in
418	  that degree by hypothesis, hence mono on homology. Being epi and mono in every
419	  degree, \(H^i(\varphi.\tau_2)\) is an isomorphism for all \(i\), so
420	  \(\varphi.\tau_2\) is a quasi-isomorphism.
421	\end{proof}

424	[The middle complex resolves \(B\)]
425	  \label{lem:horseshoe_resolvesMiddle}
426	  \lean{CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.ofShortExact_resolvesMiddle,

434	  resolution of \(B\): its terms are injective

445	  splittings (Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_degree_split}) gives a short exact sequence of

478	  short exact sequence of cochain complexes
479	  \[

481	  \]

499	  Homological Algebra}, Lemma 2.2.8), assembled from the sub-lemmas of \S``The horseshoe
500	  construction, step by step''. Take \(I_A, I_C\) and set \(I_B^n := I_A^n \oplus I_C^n\):
501	  injective by Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_biprod_injective}, degreewise split by
502	  Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_degree_split}. The twist \(\tau\) and augmentation exist with
503	  the cocycle identity (Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_twist}, via
504	  Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_stage_mono}); the matrix differential then squares to zero
505	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_dComp}) with coprojection and projection chain maps
506	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_chainMap}); and \(I_B^\bullet\) resolves \(B\)
507	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_resolvesMiddle}). These data are exactly the asserted

512	\section{The dimension-shift step}
513	
514	The induction driving the comparison theorem rests on a single short-exact-sequence
515	calculation: cutting a resolution into short exact sequences and reading off the
516	long exact sequence of right-derived functors. The latter is not available off the
517	shelf at the level of the derived-functor objects \((R^k G)\); we build it from the
518	complex-level homology long exact sequence
519	(Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) via the horseshoe lift
520	(Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}). We first record the shift at the
521	\emph{resolution} level — where the horseshoe lift directly supplies the
522	degreewise-split short exact sequence of injective resolutions — and then specialize
523	to objects.
524	
525	\begin{lemma}[Resolution-level dimension shift]
526	  \label{lem:right_derived_shift_split_resolution}
527	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.rightDerivedShiftIsoOfSplitResolutionSES}
528	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_injective_resolution,
529	    lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}
530	  Let \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) be additive and let
531	  \(0 \to A \to J \to Z \to 0\) be a short exact sequence with \(J\) right-\(G\)-acyclic,
532	  lifted to a \emph{degreewise-split} short exact sequence of injective resolutions
533	  \(0 \to I_A \to I_J \to I_Z \to 0\) (chain maps \(\varphi, \psi\) with
534	  \(\psi \circ \varphi = 0\) and a splitting of \(0 \to I_A^n \to I_J^n \to I_Z^n \to 0\)
535	  in each degree \(n\)). Then for every \(k \geq 0\) the connecting map of the homology
536	  long exact sequence of \(G(I_\bullet)\) is an isomorphism
537	  \[
538	    (R^{k+1} G)(Z) \;\cong\; (R^{k+2} G)(A).
539	  \]
540	\end{lemma}
541	
542	\begin{proof}
543	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_injective_resolution,
544	    lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}
545	  Applying \(G\) degreewise to the degreewise-split sequence preserves each split
546	  degree, yielding a short exact sequence of cochain complexes
547	  \(0 \to G(I_A) \to G(I_J) \to G(I_Z) \to 0\) in \(\mathcal{B}\). Its homology long
548	  exact sequence (Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) has connecting maps
549	  \(\delta^m : H^m(G(I_Z)) \to H^{m+1}(G(I_A))\); transport these across the
550	  injective-resolution computation \(H^m(G(I_-)) \cong (R^m G)(-)\)
551	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}). For \(m = k+1 \geq 1\) the two
552	  homology objects flanking \(\delta^m\) in the long exact sequence both sit on the
553	  middle complex \(G(I_J)\), namely \(H^{k+1}(G(I_J))\) and \(H^{k+2}(G(I_J))\); both
554	  vanish because \(J\) is right-\(G\)-acyclic (Definition~\ref{def:right_acyclic}).
555	  With both neighbours zero, \(\delta^{k+1}\) is an isomorphism, giving
556	  \((R^{k+1} G)(Z) \cong (R^{k+2} G)(A)\).
557	\end{proof}

587	  Let \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) be an additive left-exact functor, and let
588	  \[

603	% The Stacks proof of Tag 015D derives the long exact sequence from the
604	% delta-functor structure of $RF$ on the derived category $D^{+}(\mathcal{A})$.
605	% The proof below constructs the same sequence at the level of right-derived-functor
606	% objects from the complex-level homology long exact sequence
607	% (Lemma \ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) after a horseshoe lift
608	% (Lemma \ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}); accordingly no verbatim proof
609	% quote from Tag 015D is attached.
610	\begin{proof}
611	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self,
612	    lem:right_derived_injective_resolution, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence,
613	    lem:injective_resolution_of_ses, lem:right_derived_shift_split_resolution}
614	    \leanok
615	  We first construct the long exact sequence of right-derived functors associated
616	  to \(0 \to A \to J \to Z \to 0\). In the classical treatment this sequence is
617	  supplied by the \(\delta\)-functor structure of \(\{R^k G\}\) on the derived
618	  category; here it is built directly from the complex-level homology long exact
619	  sequence via a horseshoe lift.
620	
621	  \emph{Building the long exact sequence.} By the horseshoe lift
622	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}) choose injective resolutions
623	  \(I_A, I_J, I_Z\) of \(A, J, Z\) fitting into a short exact sequence of cochain
624	  complexes
625	  \[
626	    0 \longrightarrow I_A \longrightarrow I_J \longrightarrow I_Z \longrightarrow 0
627	  \]
628	  that is degreewise split. Apply \(G\) degreewise. Because each degree
629	  \(0 \to I_A^n \to I_J^n \to I_Z^n \to 0\) is a \emph{split} short exact sequence
630	  (so \(I_J^n \cong I_A^n \oplus I_Z^n\) compatibly with the maps), the additive
631	  functor \(G\) carries it to a split short exact sequence
632	  \(0 \to G(I_A^n) \to G(I_J^n) \to G(I_Z^n) \to 0\) --- this is where degreewise
633	  splitness is essential, since \(G\) is not assumed exact and would not in general
634	  preserve a non-split short exact sequence. Hence
635	  \[
636	    0 \longrightarrow G(I_A) \longrightarrow G(I_J) \longrightarrow G(I_Z)
637	      \longrightarrow 0
638	  \]
639	  is a short exact sequence of cochain complexes in \(\mathcal{B}\). The
640	  complex-level homology long exact sequence
641	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) applied to it reads
642	  \[
643	    \cdots \to H^k\bigl(G(I_J)\bigr) \to H^k\bigl(G(I_Z)\bigr)
644	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^k\ } H^{k+1}\bigl(G(I_A)\bigr)
645	      \to H^{k+1}\bigl(G(I_J)\bigr) \to \cdots .
646	  \]
647	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution} the homology of each applied
648	  resolution computes the right-derived functor:
649	  \(H^k(G(I_A)) \cong (R^k G)(A)\), \(H^k(G(I_J)) \cong (R^k G)(J)\) and
650	  \(H^k(G(I_Z)) \cong (R^k G)(Z)\), naturally in the maps of the sequence. Transporting
651	  the homology long exact sequence across these isomorphisms gives the long exact
652	  sequence of right-derived functors
653	  \[
654	    \cdots \to (R^k G)(J) \to (R^k G)(Z)
655	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^k\ } (R^{k+1} G)(A) \to (R^{k+1} G)(J) \to \cdots,
656	  \]
657	  and, using \(R^0 G \cong G\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}) at the
658	  bottom, its low-degree initial segment
659	  \[
660	    0 \to G(A) \to G(J) \to G(Z)
661	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^0\ } (R^1 G)(A) \to (R^1 G)(J) \to \cdots .
662	  \]
663	
664	  \emph{Killing the acyclic terms.} Because \(J\) is right-\(G\)-acyclic
665	  (Definition~\ref{def:right_acyclic}), every term \((R^k G)(J)\) with \(k \geq 1\)
666	  vanishes. For \(k \geq 1\) the segment around \((R^k G)(J)\),
667	  \[
668	    \underbrace{(R^{k} G)(J)}_{=0} \to (R^{k} G)(Z)
669	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^k\ } (R^{k+1} G)(A) \to \underbrace{(R^{k+1} G)(J)}_{=0},
670	  \]
671	  has both outer terms zero, so the connecting map
672	  \(\delta^k : (R^{k} G)(Z) \to (R^{k+1} G)(A)\) is an isomorphism, which is the
673	  asserted dimension shift. (The lowest-degree segment of the same long exact
674	  sequence yields the cokernel description of \((R^1 G)(A)\); that reading is
675	  recorded separately in Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_one_iso_coker}.)
676	\end{proof}

699	  (\texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses}).}

715	    lem:right_derived_injective_resolution, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence,
716	    lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}

722	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}) choose injective resolutions
723	  \(I_A, I_J, I_Z\) of \(A, J, Z\) fitting into a degreewise-split short exact
724	  sequence of cochain complexes \(0 \to I_A \to I_J \to I_Z \to 0\). Applying the
725	  additive functor \(G\) degreewise preserves each split degree, giving a short exact
726	  sequence of complexes \(0 \to G(I_A) \to G(I_J) \to G(I_Z) \to 0\) in
727	  \(\mathcal{B}\); its complex-level homology long exact sequence
728	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}), transported across the

771	  be an exact resolution of \(A\) with every \(J^n\) right-\(G\)-acyclic. Define the
772	  cosyzygies \(Z^0 := A\) and, for \(m \geq 1\),
773	  \[
774	    Z^m \;:=\; \ker\bigl(J^m \to J^{m+1}\bigr)
775	      \;=\; \operatorname{im}\bigl(J^{m-1} \to J^m\bigr).
776	  \]
777	  Then for every \(m \geq 0\) the sequence
778	  \[
779	    0 \longrightarrow Z^m \longrightarrow J^m \longrightarrow Z^{m+1}
780	      \longrightarrow 0 \tag{$S_m$}
781	  \]
782	  is short exact, with the inclusion \(Z^m \hookrightarrow J^m\) the canonical one,
783	  the surjection \(J^m \twoheadrightarrow Z^{m+1}\) the corestriction of
784	  \(J^m \to J^{m+1}\) onto its image, and the middle term \(J^m\) right-\(G\)-acyclic.
785	  In particular \(Z^1 = \operatorname{im}(J^0 \to J^1) = \operatorname{coker}(A \to J^0)\).

788	\begin{proof}
789	  \uses{def:right_acyclic}
790	  Fix \(m \geq 0\). Mono of \(Z^m \hookrightarrow J^m\) is immediate: \(Z^m\) is by
791	  definition a subobject of \(J^m\) (for \(m = 0\) via the injection \(A \hookrightarrow
792	  J^0\) of the resolution, for \(m \geq 1\) as the kernel \(\ker(J^m \to J^{m+1})\)).
793	  The map \(J^m \to Z^{m+1}\) is the corestriction of the differential
794	  \(d^m : J^m \to J^{m+1}\) onto its image \(Z^{m+1} = \operatorname{im}(d^m)\), hence
795	  an epimorphism. Exactness in the middle is exactness of the resolution at \(J^m\):
796	  the image of \(Z^m \hookrightarrow J^m\) equals \(\ker(d^m)\) --- for \(m \geq 1\)
797	  by the definition \(Z^m = \ker(J^m \to J^{m+1})\), and for \(m = 0\) because
798	  exactness of \(0 \to A \to J^0 \to J^1\) makes \(A \hookrightarrow J^0\) identify
799	  \(A\) with \(\ker(d^0)\) --- while the kernel of the epimorphism
800	  \(J^m \twoheadrightarrow Z^{m+1}\) is exactly \(\ker(d^m)\). Thus
801	  \(\operatorname{im}(Z^m \to J^m) = \ker(J^m \to Z^{m+1})\), so \((S_m)\) is short
802	  exact. The middle term \(J^m\) is right-\(G\)-acyclic by hypothesis. The final
803	  identity \(Z^1 = \operatorname{im}(J^0 \to J^1) = \operatorname{coker}(A \to J^0)\)
804	  is the case \(m = 0\): \(\operatorname{coker}(A \to J^0) = \operatorname{coker}(Z^0 \to J^0)
805	  \cong Z^1\) by \((S_0)\).
806	\end{proof}

819	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 015D
820	  (\texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses}), left-exact initial segment.}
821	  With \(G\) additive left exact and the acyclic resolution and cosyzygies of
822	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cosyzygy_ses}, for every \(n \geq 0\) the morphism \(G\) applies to
823	  the inclusion \(Z^n \hookrightarrow J^n\) to give an isomorphism
824	  \[
825	    G(Z^n) \;\xrightarrow{\ \cong\ }\; \ker\bigl(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})\bigr),
826	  \]
827	  identifying \(G(Z^n)\), as a subobject of \(G(J^n)\), with the \(n\)-th cocycle
828	  object of the applied complex \(G(J^\bullet)\). For \(n = 0\) this reads
829	  \(G(A) \cong \ker(G(J^0) \to G(J^1)) = H^0\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr)\).

832	\begin{proof}
833	  \uses{lem:cosyzygy_ses, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}
834	  Fix \(n \geq 0\). Apply the left-exact functor \(G\) to the cosyzygy short exact
835	  sequence \((S_n)\), \(0 \to Z^n \to J^n \to Z^{n+1} \to 0\) of
836	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cosyzygy_ses}; left-exactness yields an exact sequence
837	  \[
838	    0 \longrightarrow G(Z^n) \longrightarrow G(J^n) \longrightarrow G(Z^{n+1}),
839	  \]
840	  so \(G(Z^n) \hookrightarrow G(J^n)\) is the kernel of \(G(J^n) \to G(Z^{n+1})\).
841	  Next, the differential \(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})\) of the applied complex factors as
842	  \(G(J^n) \xrightarrow{G(J^n \twoheadrightarrow Z^{n+1})} G(Z^{n+1})
843	  \xrightarrow{G(Z^{n+1} \hookrightarrow J^{n+1})} G(J^{n+1})\), because the original
844	  differential \(J^n \to J^{n+1}\) factors through its image \(Z^{n+1}\) as
845	  \(J^n \twoheadrightarrow Z^{n+1} \hookrightarrow J^{n+1}\) and \(G\) preserves
846	  composition. The second factor \(G(Z^{n+1}) \to G(J^{n+1})\) is a monomorphism (the
847	  image of the left-exact application of \(G\) to \((S_{n+1})\), whose first map
848	  \(G(Z^{n+1}) \hookrightarrow G(J^{n+1})\) is mono). A monomorphism does not change
849	  kernels under precomposition, so
850	  \(\ker(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})) = \ker(G(J^n) \to G(Z^{n+1})) = G(Z^n)\). Hence
851	  \(G(Z^n) \xrightarrow{\cong} \ker(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1}))\) as subobjects of
852	  \(G(J^n)\). For \(n = 0\), \(Z^0 = A\) gives
853	  \(G(A) \cong \ker(G(J^0) \to G(J^1)) = H^0(G(J^\bullet))\), the last equality being
854	  the definition of the zeroth cohomology of a cochain complex starting in degree
855	  \(0\) (no incoming differential). The use of \(R^0 G \cong G\)
856	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}) is what licenses reading \(G(A)\) as
857	  \((R^0 G)(A)\) when this identification feeds the comparison theorem.
858	\end{proof}

860	\begin{lemma}[Cohomology of the applied resolution as a cosyzygy cokernel]
861	  \label{lem:cohomology_of_applied_resolution}
862	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.cohomologyAppliedResolutionIso}
863	  \uses{lem:cosyzygy_ses, lem:applied_cosyzygy_cycles, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}
864	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Proposition
865	  % \texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics} (Tag 05TA) and Lemma
866	  % \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses} (Tag 015D)
867	  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5785--5838 and L5619--5654).
868	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Setting $J^n = \Im(d^n)$ we break
869	  % $I^\bullet$ into short exact sequences
870	  % $0 \to J^n \to I^{n + 1} \to J^{n + 1} \to 0$
871	  % for $n \geq n_0$."
872	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 05TA
873	  (\texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics}) and Tag 015D
874	  (\texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses}).}
875	  With \(G\) additive left exact and the acyclic resolution and cosyzygies of
876	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cosyzygy_ses}, the cohomology of the applied complex
877	  \(G(J^\bullet) = \bigl(G(J^0) \to G(J^1) \to \cdots\bigr)\) is computed by
878	  \[
879	    H^0\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr) \;\cong\; G(A),
880	    \qquad
881	    H^n\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr) \;\cong\;
882	      \operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)\bigr) \quad (n \geq 1),
883	  \]
884	  where \(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)\) is \(G\) applied to the surjection
885	  \(J^{n-1} \twoheadrightarrow Z^n\) of \((S_{n-1})\).
886	\end{lemma}
887	
888	\begin{proof}
889	  \uses{lem:cosyzygy_ses, lem:applied_cosyzygy_cycles, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}
890	  The degree-zero statement is the \(n = 0\) case of
891	  Lemma~\ref{lem:applied_cosyzygy_cycles}: \(H^0(G(J^\bullet)) = \ker(G(J^0) \to G(J^1))
892	  \cong G(Z^0) = G(A)\). Fix now \(n \geq 1\). By definition of the cohomology of the
893	  cochain complex \(G(J^\bullet)\) in positive degree,
894	  \[
895	    H^n\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr)
896	      \;=\; \ker\bigl(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})\bigr) \big/
897	            \operatorname{im}\bigl(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(J^n)\bigr).
898	  \]
899	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:applied_cosyzygy_cycles} the numerator is
900	  \(\ker(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})) \cong G(Z^n)\), realized as the subobject
901	  \(G(Z^n) \hookrightarrow G(J^n)\). Under this identification the differential
902	  \(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(J^n)\) lands inside \(G(Z^n)\): it factors as
903	  \(G(J^{n-1}) \xrightarrow{G(J^{n-1} \twoheadrightarrow Z^n)} G(Z^n)
904	  \hookrightarrow G(J^n)\) by the factorization of \(J^{n-1} \to J^n\) through its image
905	  \(Z^n\), so its image equals the image of \(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)\) inside the
906	  subobject \(G(Z^n)\). Therefore the quotient
907	  \(\ker(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})) / \operatorname{im}(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(J^n))\) is exactly
908	  \(G(Z^n) / \operatorname{im}(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)) =
909	  \operatorname{coker}(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n))\). Hence
910	  \(H^n(G(J^\bullet)) \cong \operatorname{coker}(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n))\).
911	\end{proof}

915	\begin{theorem}[An acyclic resolution computes the right-derived functor]
916	  \label{lem:acyclic_resolution_computes_derived}
917	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.rightDerivedIsoOfAcyclicResolution}
918	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:cosyzygy_ses, lem:cohomology_of_applied_resolution,
919	    lem:acyclic_dimension_shift, lem:acyclic_one_iso_coker,
920	    lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self, lem:right_derived_vanishes_injective}
921	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Lemma
922	  % \texttt{lemma-leray-acyclicity} (Tag 015E, Leray's acyclicity lemma) and
923	  % Proposition \texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics} (Tag 05TA)
924	  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5692--5705 and
925	  % L5785--5811).
926	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $F : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}$ be an additive functor
927	  % between abelian categories. Let $A^\bullet$ be a bounded below complex
928	  % of right $F$-acyclic objects such that $RF$ is defined at
929	  % $A^\bullet$. The canonical map
930	  % $$
931	  % F(A^\bullet) \longrightarrow RF(A^\bullet)
932	  % $$
933	  % is an isomorphism in $D^{+}(\mathcal{B})$, i.e., $A^\bullet$ computes
934	  % $RF$."
935	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $F : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}$ be an additive functor of
936	  % abelian categories. ... If every object of $\mathcal{A}$ injects into an object
937	  % acyclic for $RF$, then $RF$ is defined on all of $K^{+}(\mathcal{A})$
938	  % and we obtain an exact functor
939	  % $$
940	  % RF : D^{+}(\mathcal{A}) \longrightarrow D^{+}(\mathcal{B})
941	  % $$
942	  % ... Moreover, any bounded below complex
943	  % $A^\bullet$ whose terms are acyclic for $RF$ computes $RF$."
944	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 015E (Leray's acyclicity
945	  lemma) and Tag 05TA (\texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics}).}
946	  Let \(\mathcal{A}\) be an abelian category with enough injectives, \(\mathcal{B}\)
947	  abelian, and \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) an additive left-exact functor.
948	  Let
949	  \[
950	    0 \longrightarrow A
951	      \longrightarrow J^0 \longrightarrow J^1 \longrightarrow J^2
952	      \longrightarrow \cdots
953	  \]
954	  be a resolution of \(A\) (an exact sequence) in which every object \(J^n\) is
955	  right-\(G\)-acyclic in the sense of Definition~\ref{def:right_acyclic}. Then for
956	  every \(n \in \mathbb{N}\) the \(n\)-th right-derived functor of \(G\) at \(A\) is
957	  computed by the resolution: there is an isomorphism in \(\mathcal{B}\)
958	  \[
959	    (R^n G)(A) \;\cong\; H^n\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr),
960	  \]
961	  where \(G(J^\bullet)\) is the cochain complex
962	  \(G(J^0) \to G(J^1) \to G(J^2) \to \cdots\) obtained by applying \(G\) degreewise
963	  to the augmentation-dropped resolution and \(H^n\) is its \(n\)-th cohomology
964	  object.
965	\end{theorem}

967	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Say $I^n = 0$ for $n < n_0$. Setting $J^n = \Im(d^n)$ we break
968	% $I^\bullet$ into short exact sequences
969	% $0 \to J^n \to I^{n + 1} \to J^{n + 1} \to 0$
970	% for $n \geq n_0$. These sequences induce distinguished triangles
971	% $(J^n, I^{n + 1}, J^{n + 1})$ in $D^+(\mathcal{A})$ by
972	% Lemma \ref{lemma-derived-canonical-delta-functor}.
973	% ... By Proposition \ref{proposition-derived-functor} we have a
974	% distinguished triangle $(RF(J^n), RF(I^{n + 1}), RF(J^{n + 1}))$.
975	% Since $J^n, I^{n + 1}, J^{n + 1}$ are in $\mathcal{I}$
976	% the long exact cohomology sequence
977	% ... associated to this distinguished triangle collapses
978	% to an exact sequence
979	% $$
980	% 0 \to F(J^n) \to F(I^{n + 1}) \to F(J^{n + 1}) \to 0
981	% $$
982	% This in turn proves that $F(I^\bullet)$ is exact."
983	\begin{proof}
984	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:cosyzygy_ses, lem:cohomology_of_applied_resolution,
985	    lem:acyclic_dimension_shift, lem:acyclic_one_iso_coker,
986	    lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self, lem:right_derived_vanishes_injective}
987	  The argument is the classical dimension shift, now assembled from the cosyzygy
988	  infrastructure and the dimension-shift step proved above. Cut the resolution into
989	  its cosyzygy short exact sequences \((S_m) : 0 \to Z^m \to J^m \to Z^{m+1} \to 0\)
990	  with \(Z^0 = A\) and acyclic middle terms, as furnished by
991	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cosyzygy_ses}.
992	
993	  \emph{Degree zero.} By Lemma~\ref{lem:cohomology_of_applied_resolution},
994	  \(H^0(G(J^\bullet)) \cong G(A)\), and by \(R^0 G \cong G\)
995	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}) the right-hand side is \((R^0 G)(A)\).
996	  This is the case \(n = 0\).
997	
998	  \emph{Positive degrees.} Fix \(n \geq 1\). The sequences
999	  \((S_0), \ldots, (S_{n-2})\) all have acyclic middle terms, so the dimension-shift
1000	  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift} applied to them gives, for each \(k \geq 1\),
1001	  isomorphisms \((R^k G)(Z^{m+1}) \cong (R^{k+1} G)(Z^m)\) for \(0 \le m \le n-2\)
1002	  (with \(Z^0 = A\)). Composing them down the staircase
1003	  \[
1004	    (R^n G)(A)
1005	      \;\cong\; (R^{n-1} G)(Z^1)
1006	      \;\cong\; (R^{n-2} G)(Z^2)
1007	      \;\cong\; \cdots
1008	      \;\cong\; (R^{1} G)(Z^{n-1})
1009	  \]
1010	  reduces \((R^n G)(A)\) to a single first-derived functor. (For \(n = 1\) the
1011	  staircase is empty and \((R^1 G)(A) = (R^1 G)(Z^0)\).) Now apply the lowest-degree
1012	  cokernel description (Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_one_iso_coker}) to \((S_{n-1})\),
1013	  \(0 \to Z^{n-1} \to J^{n-1} \to Z^n \to 0\), whose middle term \(J^{n-1}\) is
1014	  acyclic:
1015	  \[
1016	    (R^1 G)(Z^{n-1}) \;\cong\; \operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)\bigr).
1017	  \]
1018	  Finally Lemma~\ref{lem:cohomology_of_applied_resolution} identifies this cokernel
1019	  with the cohomology of the applied complex,
1020	  \(\operatorname{coker}(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)) \cong H^n(G(J^\bullet))\). Composing
1021	  the staircase, the cokernel description, and this identification gives
1022	  \((R^n G)(A) \cong H^n(G(J^\bullet))\) for every \(n \geq 1\); together with the
1023	  degree-zero case this proves the theorem for every \(n \in \mathbb{N}\).
1024	
1025	  Every long exact sequence consumed above is the one produced by the dimension-shift
1026	  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift} and its lowest-degree companion
1027	  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_one_iso_coker}, and is therefore grounded --- through the
1028	  horseshoe lift and the complex-level homology long exact sequence on which those
1029	  lemmas rest --- on the injective-resolution computation of \(R^n G\). The injective
1030	  objects supplied by ``enough injectives'' are themselves right-\(G\)-acyclic
1031	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_vanishes_injective}), which is what makes an injective
1032	  resolution a special case of an acyclic resolution and reconciles the present
1033	  formula with the defining computation \((R^n G)(A) \cong H^n(G(I^\bullet))\) for an
1034	  injective resolution \(I^\bullet\).
1035	\end{proof}

12	## Question
13	To build "an acyclic resolution computes the right-derived functor" (Leray
14	acyclicity, Stacks 015E) at the level of `CategoryTheory.Functor.rightDerived n`,
15	the kernel step is a dimension shift across a SES `0 → A → J → Z → 0` (J right-G-
16	acyclic) producing `(R^k G)(Z) ≅ (R^{k+1} G)(A)` (k ≥ 1) and
17	`(R^1 G)(A) ≅ coker(G J → G Z)`. This needs a SES → LES of right-derived functors.
18	Is the explicit horseshoe (`InjectiveResolution.ofShortExact`) the cheapest Mathlib-
19	aligned path, or does Mathlib already provide SES → LES of derived functors another
20	way (derived-category triangle, snake on a single resolution, or an Ext-level LES
21	that specializes to `rightDerived`)?

32	### Decision: how to obtain the SES → LES of `Functor.rightDerived n G`
33	
34	- **Mathlib idiom**: There is **no** SES → LES for `Functor.rightDerived n`. The
35	  closest analogues are NOT applicable:
36	  - *Ext LES* (`Mathlib/Algebra/Homology/DerivedCategory/Ext/ExactSequences.lean`,
37	    `covariantSequence` / `contravariantSequence`, `covariant_sequence_exact₁/₂/₃`).
38	    These are built from the cohomological functors `preadditiveCoyoneda` /
39	    `preadditiveYoneda` applied to `triangleOfSES`'s homology sequence. They derive
40	    the LES of `Ext^n(X, –)` / `Ext^n(–, Y)` — i.e. of **derived Hom** — NOT of a
41	    general additive functor `G`. The project's `G = f_*` is not a Hom-functor, so
42	    this LES cannot be specialized to it. (`Ext.lean` defines `Ext` as
43	    `Abelian.Ext`, derived from `Hom` in the derived category — orthogonal to a
44	    user-supplied `G`.)
45	  - *Derived-category triangulated route* (`triangleOfSES` +
46	    `triangleOfSES_distinguished` in
47	    `Mathlib/Algebra/Homology/DerivedCategory/ShortExact.lean`, plus
48	    `Pretriangulated.homologySequence_exact₁/₂/₃`). This gives the LES for a
49	    triangulated functor between derived categories. To feed `Functor.rightDerived n
50	    G` into it one needs the bridge
51	    `F.rightDerived n ≅ Hⁿ ∘ F.rightDerivedFunctorPlus ∘ Q` with
52	    `F.rightDerivedFunctorPlus : DerivedCategory.Plus C ⥤ DerivedCategory.Plus D`.
53	    **That bridge does not exist** — it is an explicit TODO in the module docstring
54	    of `Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Abelian/RightDerived.lean` (lines 40–47):
55	    "refactor `Functor.rightDerived` … when the necessary material enters mathlib:
56	    derived categories, injective/projective derivability structures, existence of
57	    derived functors from derivability structures. Eventually … redefined using
58	    `F.rightDerivedFunctorPlus`." Building it requires the injective derivability
59	    structure + existence-of-derived-functor machinery (the
60	    `CategoryTheory/Localization/DerivabilityStructure/*` + `Functor/Derived/*`
61	    framework) AND a comparison theorem — far more than the horseshoe.
62	- **Project's current path**: build `InjectiveResolution.ofShortExact` (dual
63	  Horseshoe Lemma) → apply G degreewise (split ⇒ preserved) → complex-level homology
64	  LES (`ShortComplex.ShortExact.δ/homology_exact₁/₂/₃`, present in
65	  `Mathlib/Algebra/Homology/HomologySequence.lean:282–314`) → transport along
66	  `InjectiveResolution.isoRightDerivedObj` (present).
67	- **Gap**: NEEDS_MATHLIB_GAP_FILL. The LES is genuinely absent for general `G`; the
68	  horseshoe is one self-contained way to manufacture it. The two "shortcut"
69	  alternatives are each strictly more expensive (Ext: wrong generality, unusable;
70	  derived-category: an explicitly-deferred Mathlib TODO).
71	- **Cost of divergence (if any)**: none — the horseshoe is the *cheaper* gap. The
72	  derived-category route would force the project to build (or wait for) the entire
73	  `rightDerivedFunctorPlus` bridge.
74	- **Verdict**: NEEDS_MATHLIB_GAP_FILL (horseshoe is the right gap).

76	### Decision: shape of `IsRightAcyclic`
77	
78	- **Mathlib idiom**: vanishing predicates over an object are `class … : Prop`
79	  (cf. `Injective`, `Projective` in
80	  `Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Preadditive/Injective/Basic.lean`). The companion
81	  vanishing fact is `Functor.isZero_rightDerived_obj_injective_succ` which states
82	  `IsZero ((F.rightDerived (n+1)).obj J)` for injective `J` — i.e. the
83	  index-shifted form `∀ k, IsZero ((R^{k+1} G) J)`.
84	- **Project's current path**: blueprint `def:right_acyclic` adopts exactly the
85	  index-shifted vanishing `(R^{k+1} G)(J) = 0 ∀ k`.
86	- **Gap**: identical (good). A `class IsRightAcyclic (G) (J) : Prop` wrapping
87	  `∀ k, IsZero ((G.rightDerived (k+1)).obj J)` mirrors Mathlib, and an `instance`
88	  deriving it for injectives from `isZero_rightDerived_obj_injective_succ` is free.
89	- **Verdict**: PROCEED (align field shape with the `isZero …_succ` quantifier).

91	## Recommendation
92	
93	Scaffold the **horseshoe** (`InjectiveResolution.ofShortExact`) — it is the right,
94	necessary, self-contained project-side gap, not a detour. Do NOT scaffold a derived-
95	category route: its prerequisite (`rightDerivedFunctorPlus` + injective derivability
96	structure) is an open Mathlib TODO and dwarfs the horseshoe. Do NOT try to specialize
97	the Ext LES: it is derived-Hom-specific and cannot see a general `G = f_*`.

99	Concrete scaffold shape for the horseshoe and downstream:
100	1. `IsRightAcyclic G J : Prop` as a `class` = `∀ k, IsZero ((G.rightDerived (k+1)).obj J)`;
101	   instance for `[Injective J]` via `isZero_rightDerived_obj_injective_succ`.
102	2. `InjectiveResolution.ofShortExact` — the hard part. Build `I_B` degreewise as
103	   `I_A.cocomplex.X n ⊞ I_C.cocomplex.X n` (biproduct ⇒ injective) with the
104	   **twisted** differential `[[d_A, τ], [0, d_C]]`; the off-diagonal `τ` and the
105	   augmentation `B → I_B^0` are produced stage-by-stage by the injective lifting
106	   property (`Injective.factorThru` against the relevant monos). Model the recursion
107	   on the existing `InjectiveResolution.ofCocomplex` / `exact_f_d` /
108	   `ofCocomplex_exactAt_succ` machinery
109	   (`Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Abelian/Injective/Resolution.lean:270–352`). Prove
110	   degreewise-split SES of complexes `0 → I_A → I_B → I_C → 0` and that `I_B`
111	   resolves `B` (exactness via the complex homology LES on the split SES).
112	   Expose the output as a `ShortComplex (CochainComplex C ℕ)` that `.ShortExact`.
113	3. `rightDerivedShiftIsoOfAcyclic` — apply `G` to the degreewise-split SES (additive
114	   `G` preserves split SES: use `ShortComplex.Splitting.map` /
115	   `Functor.mapShortComplex` on the per-degree splitting), get a `ShortExact` of
116	   `CochainComplex D ℕ`, feed `HomologySequence.δ` + `homology_exact₁/₂/₃`, transport
117	   each `Hⁿ(G(I_•))` to `(Rⁿ G)(•)` via `isoRightDerivedObj`, then kill the acyclic
118	   `(R^{≥1} G)(J)` terms.

121	and proving `I_B` is a resolution — a dependent recursion over `ℕ`. This is the
122	dominant risk and should get its own prover objective, scaffolded as a standalone
123	lemma chain (per-degree `τ`, chain-map laws, exactness) rather than one monolithic
124	definition. One cheap simplification to peel off first: the **injective-middle**
125	dimension shift (`0 → A → Q → A' → 0`, `Q` injective) needs NO horseshoe — it is a
126	re-indexing of a single injective resolution of `A` (shift `I_A^{≥1}`) and follows
127	from `isoRightDerivedObj` + `isZero_rightDerived_obj_injective_succ`. It does NOT
128	discharge the acyclic-middle case the cosyzygy SESs require, so it cannot replace the
129	horseshoe — but it is a useful warm-up / sanity lemma and may shorten the base degree.

example (K : CochainComplex 𝒜 ℕ) (i : ℕ) (h : K.ExactAt (i+1)) :
    Epi (K.toCycles i (i+1)) := by
  rw [HomologicalComplex.exactAt_iff] at h
  have := h.epi_toCycles
  sorry

-- check iCycles mono
example (K : CochainComplex 𝒜 ℕ) (i : ℕ) : Mono (K.iCycles i) := inferInstance

-- check the composite is zero
example (K : CochainComplex 𝒜 ℕ) (i : ℕ) :
    K.iCycles i ≫ K.toCycles i (i+1) = 0 := by
  simp

variable (i)

/-- The map `K.X i ⟶ K.cycles j` induced by the differential `K.d i j`. -/
noncomputable def toCycles [K.HasHomology j] :
    K.X i ⟶ K.cycles j :=
  K.liftCycles (K.d i j) (c.next j) rfl (K.d_comp_d _ _ _)

@[reassoc (attr := simp)]
lemma iCycles_d : K.iCycles i ≫ K.d i j = 0 := by
  by_cases hij : c.Rel i j
  · obtain rfl := c.next_eq' hij
    exact (K.sc i).iCycles_g
  · rw [K.shape _ _ hij, comp_zero]

/-- `K.cycles i` is the kernel of `K.d i j` when `c.next i = j`. -/
noncomputable def cyclesIsKernel (hj : c.next i = j) :
    IsLimit (KernelFork.ofι (K.iCycles i) (K.iCycles_d i j)) := by
  obtain rfl := hj
  exact (K.sc i).cyclesIsKernel

end

@[reassoc (attr := simp)]
lemma toCycles_i [K.HasHomology j] :
    K.toCycles i j ≫ K.iCycles j = K.d i j :=
  liftCycles_i _ _ _ _ _

section
variable [K.HasHomology i]

set_option backward.isDefEq.respectTransparency false in
instance : Mono (K.iCycles i) := by
  dsimp only [iCycles]
  infer_instance

set_option backward.isDefEq.respectTransparency false in
instance : Epi (K.homologyπ i) := by
  dsimp only [homologyπ]
  infer_instance

end

@[reassoc (attr := simp)]
lemma d_toCycles [K.HasHomology k] :
    K.d i j ≫ K.toCycles j k = 0 := by
  simp only [← cancel_mono (K.iCycles k), assoc, toCycles_i, d_comp_d, zero_comp]

variable {i j} in
lemma toCycles_eq_zero [K.HasHomology j] (hij : ¬ c.Rel i j) :
    K.toCycles i j = 0 := by
  rw [← cancel_mono (K.iCycles j), toCycles_i, zero_comp, K.shape _ _ hij]

import Mathlib
open CategoryTheory Limits
variable {𝒜 : Type*} [Category 𝒜] [Abelian 𝒜]

theorem cosyz_comp_zero (K : CochainComplex 𝒜 ℕ) (i : ℕ) :
    K.iCycles i ≫ K.toCycles i (i + 1) = 0 := by
  rw [← cancel_mono (K.iCycles (i + 1)), Category.assoc, HomologicalComplex.toCycles_i,
    HomologicalComplex.iCycles_d, zero_comp]

theorem epi_toCycles_of_exactAt (K : CochainComplex 𝒜 ℕ) (i : ℕ) (h : K.ExactAt (i+1)) :
    Epi (K.toCycles i (i+1)) := by
  rw [HomologicalComplex.exactAt_iff_isZero_homology] at h
  have hcok := K.homologyIsCokernel i (i+1) (by simp)
  rw [Preadditive.epi_iff_cancel_zero]
  intro T g hg
  set d := hcok.desc (CokernelCofork.ofπ g (by simpa using hg)) with hdd
  have hd : K.homologyπ (i+1) ≫ d = g := hcok.fac _ WalkingParallelPair.one
  have hz : K.homologyπ (i+1) = 0 := h.eq_of_tgt _ _
  rw [← hd, hz]; exact zero_comp

noncomputable def cosyzKernel (K : CochainComplex 𝒜 ℕ) (i : ℕ) :
    IsLimit (KernelFork.ofι (K.iCycles i) (cosyz_comp_zero K i)) := by
  have hk := K.cyclesIsKernel i (i+1) (by simp)
  have key : ∀ {T : 𝒜} (x : T ⟶ K.X i), x ≫ K.toCycles i (i+1) = 0 → x ≫ K.d i (i+1) = 0 := by
    intro T x hx
    rw [← K.toCycles_i i (i+1), ← Category.assoc, hx, zero_comp]
  refine KernelFork.IsLimit.ofι _ _
    (fun {T} x hx => hk.lift (KernelFork.ofι x (key x hx)))
    (fun {T} x hx => hk.fac _ WalkingParallelPair.zero)
    (fun {T} x hx m hmeq => by
      have e2 : hk.lift (KernelFork.ofι x (key x hx)) ≫ K.iCycles i = x :=
        hk.fac _ WalkingParallelPair.zero
      rw [← cancel_mono (K.iCycles i)]
      exact hmeq.trans e2.symm)

noncomputable def cosyzygySC (K : CochainComplex 𝒜 ℕ) (i : ℕ) : ShortComplex 𝒜 :=
  ShortComplex.mk (K.iCycles i) (K.toCycles i (i+1)) (cosyz_comp_zero K i)

lemma cosyzygySC_shortExact (K : CochainComplex 𝒜 ℕ) (i : ℕ) (h : K.ExactAt (i+1)) :
    (cosyzygySC K i).ShortExact where
  exact := ShortComplex.exact_of_f_is_kernel _ (cosyzKernel K i)
  mono_f := by dsimp [cosyzygySC]; infer_instance
  epi_g := epi_toCycles_of_exactAt K i h

/-! ## Project-local Mathlib supplement — cosyzygy short exact sequences

For an exact cochain complex `K` (the augmentation-dropped resolution `J⁰ → J¹ → ⋯`), the
cosyzygies `Zⁿ := K.cycles n = ker(dⁿ)` fit into short exact sequences
`(Sₙ) : 0 → Zⁿ → Kⁿ → Zⁿ⁺¹ → 0`, where the inclusion is `iCycles` and the surjection is the
corestriction `toCycles` of the differential onto the next cycles. These are the sequences cut
out of the resolution that drive the dimension-shift staircase in the comparison theorem
`rightDerivedIsoOfAcyclicResolution` (blueprint `lem:cosyzygy_ses`). The construction is purely
abelian-categorical; the right-`G`-acyclicity of the middle terms `Kⁿ` is carried as a typeclass
hypothesis only at the point of use. These declarations are not in Mathlib. -/

section Cosyzygy

omit [HasInjectiveResolutions 𝒜]

/-- The cosyzygy composite `Zⁿ ↪ Kⁿ ↠ Zⁿ⁺¹` vanishes: the cycles inclusion lands in the kernel
of the differential, hence in the kernel of its corestriction `toCycles`. -/
theorem cosyzygy_iCycles_comp_toCycles (K : CochainComplex 𝒜 ℕ) (n : ℕ) :
    K.iCycles n ≫ K.toCycles n (n + 1) = 0 := by
  rw [← cancel_mono (K.iCycles (n + 1)), Category.assoc, HomologicalComplex.toCycles_i,
    HomologicalComplex.iCycles_d, zero_comp]

/-- If the complex `K` is exact at `n + 1`, the corestriction `toCycles n (n+1) : Kⁿ → Zⁿ⁺¹` is an
epimorphism: its cokernel is the homology `Hⁿ⁺¹(K)`, which vanishes by exactness. -/
theorem epi_toCycles_of_exactAt (K : CochainComplex 𝒜 ℕ) (n : ℕ) (h : K.ExactAt (n + 1)) :
    Epi (K.toCycles n (n + 1)) := by
  rw [HomologicalComplex.exactAt_iff_isZero_homology] at h
  have hcok := K.homologyIsCokernel n (n + 1) (by simp)
  rw [Preadditive.epi_iff_cancel_zero]
  intro T g hg
  set d := hcok.desc (CokernelCofork.ofπ g (by simpa using hg)) with hdd
  have hd : K.homologyπ (n + 1) ≫ d = g := hcok.fac _ Limits.WalkingParallelPair.one
  have hz : K.homologyπ (n + 1) = 0 := h.eq_of_tgt _ _
  rw [← hd, hz]; exact zero_comp

/-- The cycles inclusion `iCycles n` is the kernel of the corestriction `toCycles n (n+1)`: both
`d n (n+1)` and `toCycles n (n+1)` have the same kernel, since `d = toCycles ≫ (mono iCycles)`. -/
noncomputable def cosyzygyKernelFork (K : CochainComplex 𝒜 ℕ) (n : ℕ) :
    Limits.IsLimit
      (Limits.KernelFork.ofι (K.iCycles n) (cosyzygy_iCycles_comp_toCycles K n)) := by
  have hk := K.cyclesIsKernel n (n + 1) (by simp)
  have key : ∀ {T : 𝒜} (x : T ⟶ K.X n),
      x ≫ K.toCycles n (n + 1) = 0 → x ≫ K.d n (n + 1) = 0 := by
    intro T x hx
    rw [← K.toCycles_i n (n + 1), ← Category.assoc, hx, zero_comp]
  refine Limits.KernelFork.IsLimit.ofι _ _
    (fun {T} x hx => hk.lift (Limits.KernelFork.ofι x (key x hx)))
    (fun {T} x hx => hk.fac _ Limits.WalkingParallelPair.zero)
    (fun {T} x hx m hmeq => by
      have e2 : hk.lift (Limits.KernelFork.ofι x (key x hx)) ≫ K.iCycles n = x :=
        hk.fac _ Limits.WalkingParallelPair.zero
      rw [← cancel_mono (K.iCycles n)]
      exact hmeq.trans e2.symm)

/-- The `n`-th cosyzygy short complex `Zⁿ → Kⁿ → Zⁿ⁺¹` (`iCycles` then `toCycles`). -/
noncomputable def Functor.cosyzygyShortComplex (K : CochainComplex 𝒜 ℕ) (n : ℕ) :
    ShortComplex 𝒜 :=
  ShortComplex.mk (K.iCycles n) (K.toCycles n (n + 1)) (cosyzygy_iCycles_comp_toCycles K n)

/-- **Cosyzygy short exact sequence** (blueprint `lem:cosyzygy_ses`). For a complex `K` exact at
`n + 1`, the cosyzygy short complex `0 → Zⁿ → Kⁿ → Zⁿ⁺¹ → 0` is short exact: the inclusion
`iCycles n` is a monomorphism and a kernel of the corestriction `toCycles n (n+1)`, which is an
epimorphism by exactness. -/
theorem Functor.cosyzygyShortComplex_shortExact (K : CochainComplex 𝒜 ℕ) (n : ℕ)
    (h : K.ExactAt (n + 1)) : (Functor.cosyzygyShortComplex K n).ShortExact where
  exact := ShortComplex.exact_of_f_is_kernel _ (cosyzygyKernelFork K n)
  mono_f := by dsimp [Functor.cosyzygyShortComplex]; infer_instance
  epi_g := epi_toCycles_of_exactAt K n h

end Cosyzygy

    (ψ : HomologyMapData φ h₁ h₂) (F : C ⥤ D) [F.PreservesZeroMorphisms]
    [h₁.left.IsPreservedBy F] [h₁.right.IsPreservedBy F]
    [h₂.left.IsPreservedBy F] [h₂.right.IsPreservedBy F] :

lemma map_leftRightHomologyComparison' (F : C ⥤ D) [F.PreservesZeroMorphisms]
    (hₗ : S.LeftHomologyData) (hᵣ : S.RightHomologyData) [hₗ.IsPreservedBy F] [hᵣ.IsPreservedBy F] :
    F.map (leftRightHomologyComparison' hₗ hᵣ) =
      leftRightHomologyComparison' (hₗ.map F) (hᵣ.map F) := by
  apply Cofork.IsColimit.hom_ext (hₗ.map F).hπ
  apply Fork.IsLimit.hom_ext (hᵣ.map F).hι
  trans F.map (hₗ.i ≫ hᵣ.p)
  · simp [← Functor.map_comp]
  trans (hₗ.map F).π ≫ ShortComplex.leftRightHomologyComparison'
    (hₗ.map F) (hᵣ.map F) ≫ (hᵣ.map F).ι
  · rw [ShortComplex.π_leftRightHomologyComparison'_ι]; simp
  · simp

end ShortComplex

namespace Functor

variable (F : C ⥤ D) [PreservesZeroMorphisms F] (S : ShortComplex C) {S₁ S₂ : ShortComplex C}

/-- A functor preserves the left homology of a short complex `S` if it preserves all the
left homology data of `S`. -/
class PreservesLeftHomologyOf : Prop where
  /-- the functor preserves all the left homology data of the short complex -/
  isPreservedBy : ∀ (h : S.LeftHomologyData), h.IsPreservedBy F

/-- A functor preserves the right homology of a short complex `S` if it preserves all the
right homology data of `S`. -/
class PreservesRightHomologyOf : Prop where
  /-- the functor preserves all the right homology data of the short complex -/
  isPreservedBy : ∀ (h : S.RightHomologyData), h.IsPreservedBy F

instance PreservesHomology.preservesLeftHomologyOf [F.PreservesHomology] :
    F.PreservesLeftHomologyOf S := ⟨inferInstance⟩

instance PreservesHomology.preservesRightHomologyOf [F.PreservesHomology] :
    F.PreservesRightHomologyOf S := ⟨inferInstance⟩

variable {S}

/-- If a functor preserves a certain left homology data of a short complex `S`, then it
preserves the left homology of `S`. -/
lemma PreservesLeftHomologyOf.mk' (h : S.LeftHomologyData) [h.IsPreservedBy F] :
    F.PreservesLeftHomologyOf S where
  isPreservedBy h' :=
    { g := ShortComplex.LeftHomologyData.IsPreservedBy.hg h F
      f' := by
        have := ShortComplex.LeftHomologyData.IsPreservedBy.hf' h F
        let e : parallelPair h.f' 0 ≅ parallelPair h'.f' 0 :=
          parallelPair.ext (Iso.refl _) (ShortComplex.cyclesMapIso' (Iso.refl S) h h')
            (by simp) (by simp)
        exact preservesColimit_of_iso_diagram F e }

/-- If a functor preserves a certain right homology data of a short complex `S`, then it
preserves the right homology of `S`. -/
lemma PreservesRightHomologyOf.mk' (h : S.RightHomologyData) [h.IsPreservedBy F] :
    F.PreservesRightHomologyOf S where
  isPreservedBy h' :=
    { f := ShortComplex.RightHomologyData.IsPreservedBy.hf h F
      g' := by
        have := ShortComplex.RightHomologyData.IsPreservedBy.hg' h F
        let e : parallelPair h.g' 0 ≅ parallelPair h'.g' 0 :=
          parallelPair.ext (ShortComplex.opcyclesMapIso' (Iso.refl S) h h') (Iso.refl _)
            (by simp) (by simp)
        exact preservesLimit_of_iso_diagram F e }

end Functor

namespace ShortComplex

variable {S : ShortComplex C} (h₁ : S.LeftHomologyData) (h₂ : S.RightHomologyData)
  (F : C ⥤ D) [F.PreservesZeroMorphisms]

instance LeftHomologyData.isPreservedBy_of_preserves [F.PreservesLeftHomologyOf S] :
    h₁.IsPreservedBy F :=
  Functor.PreservesLeftHomologyOf.isPreservedBy _

instance RightHomologyData.isPreservedBy_of_preserves [F.PreservesRightHomologyOf S] :
    h₂.IsPreservedBy F :=
  Functor.PreservesRightHomologyOf.isPreservedBy _

/-- The map of a kernel fork is a limit iff
the kernel fork consisting of the mapped morphisms is a limit.
This essentially lets us commute `KernelFork.ofι` with `Functor.mapCone`.

This is a variant of `isLimitMapConeForkEquiv` for equalizers,
which we can't use directly between `G.map 0 = 0` does not hold definitionally.
-/
def isLimitMapConeForkEquiv' :
    IsLimit (G.mapCone (KernelFork.ofι h w)) ≃
      IsLimit
        (KernelFork.ofι (G.map h) (by simp only [← G.map_comp, w, Functor.map_zero]) :
          Fork (G.map f) 0) :=
  KernelFork.isLimitMapConeEquiv _ _

/-- The property of preserving kernels expressed in terms of kernel forks.

This is a variant of `isLimitForkMapOfIsLimit` for equalizers,
which we can't use directly between `G.map 0 = 0` does not hold definitionally.
-/
def isLimitForkMapOfIsLimit' [PreservesLimit (parallelPair f 0) G]
    (l : IsLimit (KernelFork.ofι h w)) :
    IsLimit
      (KernelFork.ofι (G.map h) (by simp only [← G.map_comp, w, Functor.map_zero]) :
        Fork (G.map f) 0) :=
  isLimitMapConeForkEquiv' G w (isLimitOfPreserves G l)

variable (f)
variable [HasKernel f]

/-- If `G` preserves kernels and `C` has them, then the fork constructed of the mapped morphisms of
a kernel fork is a limit.
-/
def isLimitOfHasKernelOfPreservesLimit [PreservesLimit (parallelPair f 0) G] :
    IsLimit
      (Fork.ofι (G.map (kernel.ι f))
          (by simp only [← G.map_comp, kernel.condition, comp_zero, Functor.map_zero]) :
        Fork (G.map f) 0) :=
  isLimitForkMapOfIsLimit' G (kernel.condition f) (kernelIsKernel f)

instance [PreservesLimit (parallelPair f 0) G] : HasKernel (G.map f) where
  exists_limit := ⟨⟨_, isLimitOfHasKernelOfPreservesLimit G f⟩⟩

variable [HasKernel (G.map f)]

/-- If the kernel comparison map for `G` at `f` is an isomorphism, then `G` preserves the
kernel of `f`.
-/
lemma PreservesKernel.of_iso_comparison [i : IsIso (kernelComparison f G)] :
    PreservesLimit (parallelPair f 0) G := by
  apply preservesLimit_of_preserves_limit_cone (kernelIsKernel f)
  apply (isLimitMapConeForkEquiv' G (kernel.condition f)).symm _
  exact @IsLimit.ofPointIso _ _ _ _ _ _ _ (kernelIsKernel (G.map f)) i

variable [PreservesLimit (parallelPair f 0) G]

/-- If `G` preserves the kernel of `f`, then the kernel comparison map for `G` at `f` is
an isomorphism.
-/
def PreservesKernel.iso : G.obj (kernel f) ≅ kernel (G.map f) :=
  IsLimit.conePointUniqueUpToIso (isLimitOfHasKernelOfPreservesLimit G f) (limit.isLimit _)

import Mathlib

variable {𝒜 : Type*} [Category 𝒜] [Abelian 𝒜]
variable {ℬ : Type*} [Category ℬ] [Abelian ℬ]

noncomputable def gCosyzygyIso (G : 𝒜 ⥤ ℬ) [G.Additive] [PreservesFiniteLimits G]
    (K : CochainComplex 𝒜 ℕ) (n : ℕ) :
    G.obj (K.cycles n) ≅ ((G.mapHomologicalComplex (ComplexShape.up ℕ)).obj K).cycles n := by
  set GK := (G.mapHomologicalComplex (ComplexShape.up ℕ)).obj K with hGK
  have hd : GK.d n (n + 1) = G.map (K.d n (n + 1)) := by simp [hGK]
  have l1 := isLimitForkMapOfIsLimit' G (K.iCycles_d n (n + 1))
    (K.cyclesIsKernel n (n + 1) (by simp))
  have l2 := GK.cyclesIsKernel n (n + 1) (by simp)
  exact IsLimit.conePointUniqueUpToIso l1 (hd ▸ l2)

open CategoryTheory Limits
variable {𝒜 : Type*} [Category 𝒜] [Abelian 𝒜]
variable {ℬ : Type*} [Category ℬ] [Abelian ℬ]

-- is GK.d defeq G.map (K.d)?
example (G : 𝒜 ⥤ ℬ) [G.Additive] (K : CochainComplex 𝒜 ℕ) (n : ℕ) :
    ((G.mapHomologicalComplex (ComplexShape.up ℕ)).obj K).d n (n + 1) = G.map (K.d n (n + 1)) := by
  rfl

noncomputable def gCosyzygyIso (G : 𝒜 ⥤ ℬ) [G.Additive] [PreservesFiniteLimits G]
    (K : CochainComplex 𝒜 ℕ) (n : ℕ) :
    G.obj (K.cycles n) ≅ ((G.mapHomologicalComplex (ComplexShape.up ℕ)).obj K).cycles n := by

/-- **Applied cosyzygy is the cocycle object** (core of blueprint `lem:applied_cosyzygy_cycles`).
A finite-limit-preserving (e.g. left-exact) functor `G` carries the cycles object
`Zⁿ = K.cycles n` of a complex to the cycles object of the applied complex `G(K)` — that is, to
the `n`-th cocycle object `ker(G(dⁿ) : G(Kⁿ) → G(Kⁿ⁺¹))`. This is the left-exactness
identification `G(Zⁿ) ≅ ker(G(Jⁿ) → G(J^{n+1}))`: `G` preserves the kernel
`iCycles n` defining the cycles, transported to the canonical cycles kernel of `G(K)`. -/
noncomputable def Functor.gCyclesIso (G : 𝒜 ⥤ ℬ) [G.Additive] [Limits.PreservesFiniteLimits G]
    (K : CochainComplex 𝒜 ℕ) (n : ℕ) :
    G.obj (K.cycles n) ≅ ((G.mapHomologicalComplex (ComplexShape.up ℕ)).obj K).cycles n :=
  Limits.IsLimit.conePointUniqueUpToIso
    (Limits.isLimitForkMapOfIsLimit' G (K.iCycles_d n (n + 1))
      (K.cyclesIsKernel n (n + 1) (by simp)))
    (((G.mapHomologicalComplex (ComplexShape.up ℕ)).obj K).cyclesIsKernel n (n + 1) (by simp))

/-- The cycles iso `Functor.gCyclesIso` is compatible with the cycles inclusions: composing with the
cycles inclusion of `G(K)` recovers `G` applied to the cycles inclusion of `K`. This expresses that
the iso identifies `G(Zⁿ)`, as a subobject of `G(Kⁿ)`, with the cocycle object. -/
@[reassoc]
lemma Functor.gCyclesIso_hom_iCycles (G : 𝒜 ⥤ ℬ) [G.Additive] [Limits.PreservesFiniteLimits G]
    (K : CochainComplex 𝒜 ℕ) (n : ℕ) :
    (G.gCyclesIso K n).hom ≫ ((G.mapHomologicalComplex (ComplexShape.up ℕ)).obj K).iCycles n =
      G.map (K.iCycles n) :=
  Limits.IsLimit.conePointUniqueUpToIso_hom_comp _ _ Limits.WalkingParallelPair.zero

end Cosyzygy

    [Limits.PreservesFiniteLimits G] (K : CochainComplex 𝒜 ℕ) :

    CochainComplex.isoHomologyπ₀ ((G.mapHomologicalComplex (ComplexShape.up ℕ)).obj K)

end Cosyzygy

import Mathlib
open CategoryTheory Limits
variable {𝒜 : Type*} [Category 𝒜] [Abelian 𝒜]
variable {ℬ : Type*} [Category ℬ] [Abelian ℬ]

noncomputable def gCyclesIso (G : 𝒜 ⥤ ℬ) [G.Additive] [PreservesFiniteLimits G]
    (K : CochainComplex 𝒜 ℕ) (n : ℕ) :
    G.obj (K.cycles n) ≅ ((G.mapHomologicalComplex (ComplexShape.up ℕ)).obj K).cycles n :=
  IsLimit.conePointUniqueUpToIso
    (isLimitForkMapOfIsLimit' G (K.iCycles_d n (n + 1)) (K.cyclesIsKernel n (n + 1) (by simp)))
    (((G.mapHomologicalComplex (ComplexShape.up ℕ)).obj K).cyclesIsKernel n (n + 1) (by simp))

lemma gCyclesIso_hom_iCycles (G : 𝒜 ⥤ ℬ) [G.Additive] [PreservesFiniteLimits G]
    (K : CochainComplex 𝒜 ℕ) (n : ℕ) :
    (gCyclesIso G K n).hom ≫ ((G.mapHomologicalComplex (ComplexShape.up ℕ)).obj K).iCycles n =
      G.map (K.iCycles n) :=
  IsLimit.conePointUniqueUpToIso_hom_comp _ _ WalkingParallelPair.zero

example (G : 𝒜 ⥤ ℬ) [G.Additive] [PreservesFiniteLimits G]
    (K : CochainComplex 𝒜 ℕ) (m : ℕ) :
    G.map (K.toCycles m (m + 1)) ≫ (gCyclesIso G K (m + 1)).hom =
      ((G.mapHomologicalComplex (ComplexShape.up ℕ)).obj K).toCycles m (m + 1) := by
  rw [← cancel_mono (((G.mapHomologicalComplex (ComplexShape.up ℕ)).obj K).iCycles (m + 1)),
    Category.assoc, gCyclesIso_hom_iCycles, ← G.map_comp]
  simp

# AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/AcyclicResolution.lean

## Summary
- **Declarations added: 8** (all axiom-clean `{propext, Classical.choice, Quot.sound}`, file compiles
  with **0 sorry**). New section `Cosyzygy` (lines ~676–780), inside `namespace CategoryTheory`,
  `omit [HasInjectiveResolutions 𝒜]`.
  1. `cosyzygy_iCycles_comp_toCycles` (line 691) — helper, the cosyzygy composite `Zⁿ↪Kⁿ↠Zⁿ⁺¹ = 0`.
  2. `epi_toCycles_of_exactAt` (line 698) — helper, `ExactAt (n+1) → Epi (toCycles n (n+1))`.
  3. `cosyzygyKernelFork` (line 711) — helper, `iCycles n` is a kernel of `toCycles n (n+1)`.
  4. `Functor.cosyzygyShortComplex` (line 729) — the short complex `Zⁿ → Kⁿ → Zⁿ⁺¹` (`lean_aux`).
  5. **`Functor.cosyzygyShortExact`** (line 737) — **blueprint `lem:cosyzygy_ses`** (cosyzygy SES).
  6. **`Functor.gCosyzygyIsoCocycles`** (line 748) — **core of blueprint `lem:applied_cosyzygy_cycles`**
     (`G(Zⁿ) ≅ ker(G(Jⁿ)→G(J^{n+1}))` via `G` preserving the cycles kernel).
  7. `Functor.gCosyzygyIsoCocycles_hom_iCycles` (line 760) — inclusion-compatibility helper for (6).
  8. `Functor.gHomologyZeroIso` (line 773) — degree-`0` case of blueprint
     `lem:cohomology_of_applied_resolution` (`H⁰(G(K•)) ≅ G(Z⁰)`).
- **Declarations blocked: 3** of the 5 TARGET-3 leaves (`rightDerivedOneIsoCokerOfAcyclic`,
  the positive-degree part of `cohomologyAppliedResolutionIso`, and the assembly
  `rightDerivedIsoOfAcyclicResolution`). Reasons below.
- **sorry count: 0 → 0** across the file (no sorries before; none added).

## Design decision (input representation — I own this, no protected decl affected)
The cosyzygy infrastructure is stated for a **bare cochain complex** `K : CochainComplex 𝒜 ℕ`
(the augmentation-dropped resolution `J⁰ → J¹ → ⋯`), with exactness supplied pointwise as
`K.ExactAt (n+1)` and the cosyzygy `Zⁿ := K.cycles n` (= `ker dⁿ`). This is deliberately
G-free / augmentation-free so the SES construction is reusable; the right-`G`-acyclicity of the
middle term `Kⁿ` and the augmentation `A ≅ K.cycles 0` are layered on **at the point of use**
(both are cheap: `IsRightAcyclic (K.X n)` is an `inferInstance` from `[∀ n, IsRightAcyclic (J.X n)]`;
`A ≅ cycles 0` is the augmentation/quasi-iso datum). The eventual TARGET-3 theorem
`rightDerivedIsoOfAcyclicResolution` should take `(K) (A) (e : A ≅ K.cycles 0)
(hexact : ∀ n, K.ExactAt (n+1)) [∀ n, G.IsRightAcyclic (K.X n)] [G.Additive]
[PreservesFiniteLimits G]`. (`PreservesFiniteLimits` is the standard left-exact hypothesis,
matching Mathlib's `rightDerivedZeroIsoSelf`.)

## Blueprint `\lean{}` name alignment
- `lem:cosyzygy_ses` → renamed my lemma to **`CategoryTheory.Functor.cosyzygyShortExact`** ✓ matches.
  NOTE: blueprint statement bundles "acyclic middle"; my signature instead takes `K.ExactAt (n+1)`
  and leaves acyclicity to `inferInstance`. The DAG node links by name; the planner may want to
  tweak the blueprint prose/`\uses` to reflect the bare-complex framing.
- `lem:applied_cosyzygy_cycles` → renamed to **`CategoryTheory.Functor.gCosyzygyIsoCocycles`** ✓
  matches. My iso is `G.obj (K.cycles n) ≅ (G(K)).cycles n`; the RHS `(G(K)).cycles n` is exactly
  `ker(G(dⁿ))`, i.e. the cocycle object. n=0 gives `G(A) ≅ H⁰` after composing `e` and `gHomologyZeroIso`.

## cosyzygy SES (`Functor.cosyzygyShortExact`) — RESOLVED, axiom-clean
- **Approach:** `ShortComplex.mk (K.iCycles n) (K.toCycles n (n+1)) _`; short-exact via
  `mono_f` (instance), `epi_g = epi_toCycles_of_exactAt` (cokernel of `toCycles` is `Hⁿ⁺¹(K) = 0`),
  `exact = exact_of_f_is_kernel _ cosyzygyKernelFork`. `cosyzygyKernelFork` transports
  `K.cyclesIsKernel` (kernel of `dⁿ`) to a kernel of `toCycles` since
  `dⁿ = toCycles ≫ (mono iCycles(n+1))`.
- **Key Mathlib API:** `HomologicalComplex.{toCycles,iCycles,toCycles_i,iCycles_d,cyclesIsKernel,
  homologyIsCokernel, exactAt_iff_isZero_homology}`, `Limits.KernelFork.IsLimit.ofι`,
  `ShortComplex.exact_of_f_is_kernel`, `Preadditive.epi_iff_cancel_zero`.

## gCosyzygyIsoCocycles — RESOLVED, axiom-clean
- **Approach:** `G` preserves the finite limit `K.cyclesIsKernel n (n+1)` (kernel of `dⁿ`); the
  mapped kernel fork (`Limits.isLimitForkMapOfIsLimit'`) and the canonical
  `(G(K)).cyclesIsKernel n (n+1)` are two limits of the *same* fork `Fork (G.map dⁿ) 0`
  (the differentials are **defeq**: `(G(K)).d n (n+1) = G.map (K.d n (n+1))` by `rfl`), so
  `Limits.IsLimit.conePointUniqueUpToIso` gives `G.obj (cycles n) ≅ (G(K)).cycles n`.
- **Dead end avoided:** `ShortComplex.mapCyclesIso` requires `F.PreservesLeftHomologyOf S`, which
  needs `F` to preserve a **cokernel** too (`PreservesHomology` ⇐ finite limits **and** colimits).
  `G = f_*` is only left exact (finite limits), so that route is unavailable — use the bare
  kernel-preservation `isLimitForkMapOfIsLimit'` instead.

## Functor.rightDerivedOneIsoCokerOfAcyclic (`lem:acyclic_one_iso_coker`) — NOT ADDED
- **What it needs:** the low-degree segment `G(J)→G(Z)→(R¹G)(A)→(R¹G)(J)=0` of the homology LES of

variable {𝒜 : Type*} [Category 𝒜] [Abelian 𝒜]
variable {ℬ : Type*} [Category ℬ] [Abelian ℬ]

noncomputable def gco (G : 𝒜 ⥤ ℬ) [G.Additive] [PreservesFiniteLimits G]
    (K : CochainComplex 𝒜 ℕ) (n : ℕ) :

  IsLimit.conePointUniqueUpToIso

    (((G.mapHomologicalComplex (ComplexShape.up ℕ)).obj K).cyclesIsKernel n (n + 1) (by simp))
